title: Seeing What Changed
subtitle: Spotting differences

sections.content: Before you commit, you often want to see exactly what you changed. `git diff` shows you a line-by-line comparison between your current files and the last committed version — additions in green, deletions in red.
sections.analogy: Using `git diff` is like holding two photos side by side and playing spot-the-difference. Git highlights exactly what was added, removed, or changed so you can review your work before committing it.
keyPoints: `git diff` shows unstaged changes compared to the last commit
Added lines are shown with a `+` prefix (green), removed lines with `-` (red)
Always review your diff before committing to catch mistakes early

sections.instruction: Before we can see changes, we need a git repository with a commit to compare against.
sections.prompt: Type `git init` to create a repository.

onSuccess.message: Repository initialized. Now let's save the current version of app.js.

sections.instruction: Stage all files for your first commit.
Create the initial commit so git has a baseline to compare against.

onSuccess.message: Committed! Git now remembers exactly what app.js looks like right now.

sections.question: What does git diff compare by default?
options: Your current files vs the last commit
sections.explanation: By default, git diff shows the differences between your working directory (current files) and the last commit. It highlights what you've changed but haven't committed yet.

sections.instruction: Now let's change the file. We'll add a new line to app.js.
sections.prompt: Type `echo "console.log('Goodbye');" >> app.js` to add a line.

onSuccess.message: File modified! But you haven't staged or committed this change. Git knows something is different.

sections.instruction: Now use `git diff` to see exactly what changed since your last commit.
sections.prompt: Type `git diff` to see the difference.

onSuccess.message: Lines starting with `+` are additions, lines starting with `-` are removals. Green means added, red means removed. This is how developers review their changes before committing.

completionMessage: `git diff` shows you exactly what changed since your last commit. Use it before every commit to review your work. It's like a spell-checker for your code changes.
